I'd play Kd6. Kd6 is the move to trust here, and your choice was a good one. It improves the king without rushing into a pawn check or pawn push, and it keeps the position under control: the king steps to d6, and Black can continue with a simple plan of improving the king further, for example Kd6 Ke4 Kc6. In this kind of rook-and-pawn endgame, the king is not just a defender — it is the piece that coordinates the whole position. Takeaway: In rook-and-pawn endgames, improve the king first when there is no urgent tactic; a steady king often wins more cleanly than a forcing pawn move.

to play Be6. Your move f4 was not a disaster, but it missed a simple chance to improve your bishop. For a beginner, this is a good kind of move: it improves a piece and gives it a clear job. How to find it: When there is no hanging piece to win or defend, run this simple checklist: first ask, 'Can I improve one of my pieces?' Then ask, 'Does that move also defend something important?' Takeaway: If there is no urgent tactic, improve a piece and make sure it has a clear job, like defending an important pawn.

to play Kd1. Your f4 move is playable, but it missed a more useful king move: Kd1 attacks the bishop on d2 and also defends your pawn on f3. That is a nice endgame habit — improve your king while creating a concrete problem for the opponent. Instead of only pushing a pawn, you make Black respond to your king’s activity and keep your own pawn structure supported. How to find it: In a simplified position, run this routine before making a pawn move: first ask, “Can my king improve and attack something?” Then check, “Does that same move also defend one of my pawns?” Here, Kd1 passes both tests: it attacks the bishop on d2 and defends the pawn on f3. When one move gains activity and adds protection, it is usually more useful than a quiet pawn push. Takeaway: In endgames, look for king moves that do two jobs: attack an enemy piece and defend your own pawn.

://. Your move Rxg5 was a good practical choice: it captures the undefended pawn on g5, and your rook also defends your pawn on g7. h5. Your Rxg5 was a very natural move because it captures the attacked g5-pawn and also defends g7, but it commits the rook immediately. The more instructive move is to first use the h-pawn to attack g5, keeping the rook flexible while still putting pressure on the same pawn. This is a good conversion habit: when you can gain a tempo on a target without giving up your rook’s activity, you often keep more options for the next move. How to find it: When you see an undefended pawn you can take, do not capture automatically. Run this routine: first ask, “Can I attack the same pawn with a pawn or piece while keeping my active piece flexible?” Here, h5 attacks g5, so you gain the same pressure as Rxg5 but without committing the rook. At this level, compare the forcing move with the flexible move: if the flexible move keeps the target under pressure and preserves your piece activity, it is often the cleaner choice. Takeaway: Before taking an undefended pawn, check whether you can attack it with a pawn first and keep your active piece flexible.

How to find it: When you are not in check, run this quick opening checklist: first look for any hanging pieces, then ask, 'Which knight or bishop is still not developed?' Here, the knight on g8 is the piece to improve, so choose a developing move like Nh6 instead of making a random pawn move. Takeaway: If there is no urgent threat, develop an undeveloped knight or bishop before making random pawn moves.

4+ is a tempting check, but it misses the more useful developing move: Ne7. Your move f6 is a bit of a pawn move that does not improve a piece, while Ne7 develops the knight from g8 and gives it a clear job: defending the bishop on c8 and the pawn on c6. This is a good intermediate-level move because it improves your position without needing a forcing reply. How to find it: When you are tempted to play a check or a pawn move, pause and run this routine: first ask, 'Do I have an undeveloped piece that can improve with a useful job?' Here, the knight on g8 is the piece to notice. Then ask, 'Can I develop it while also defending something important?' Ne7 passes that test because it develops the knight and defends c8 and c6. Takeaway: Before playing a check or a pawn move, look for a developing move that also improves your coordination or defends an important piece.

9Nh6. Your f6 idea is a bit too committal because it spends a tempo on a pawn move while your knight on g8 is still undeveloped. Nh6 is the more instructive move: it develops a minor piece and adds protection to f7, so you improve your position without loosening your structure or giving White an easy target. The key is that you first solve the coordination issue, then you can look for active queen or bishop moves like Qh4+ or Bb6 with a more stable setup. Takeaway: In a closed or semi-closed center, improve your worst-placed piece before committing to pawn moves that may only give the opponent a clearer target.

Your Qd7 move is a quiet queen move, but it missed a simple chance to make White respond: your d5 pawn can attack the bishop on c4. This is a good beginner idea because it develops your center and creates a one-move threat instead of just moving the queen. How to find it: When you are choosing a quiet move, first scan your center pawns and ask: 'Can one of them move forward and attack an enemy piece?' Here, the d5 pawn can move to d5 and attack the bishop on c4, so you should look at that before making a slower queen move. Takeaway: Before making a quiet queen move, check whether a center pawn can move forward and attack an enemy piece.

7 — your Qd7 move is a bit passive here because it misses the chance to challenge White’s bishop on c4. The move d5 does that directly, attacks the bishop, and also puts pressure on the pawn on e4. Instead of making a quiet queen move, you gain time by improving your central pawn and making White respond to your threat. How to find it: When you are choosing between a quiet move and a central pawn move, first ask: 'Can I challenge an active enemy piece while also attacking a pawn or improving my center?' Here, the bishop on c4 is a clear target, and d5 attacks it while also hitting e4. That is the kind of move you should look for before making a slower move like Qd7. Takeaway: Before making a quiet move, look for a central pawn move that attacks an active enemy piece and creates a second target.

Your Qd7 idea is understandable as a consolidating move, but it missed the more forcing way to use the position: your d5 pawn can attack the bishop on c4 and the pawn on e4 at the same time. That matters because you gain a tempo on an active bishop while also challenging White’s central pawn, so White has to respond to your pressure instead of freely improving. How to find it: When you have a central pawn that can move with tempo, pause before making a quiet consolidating move and ask: “Can this pawn attack a piece and a pawn at the same time?” Here, d5 passes that test because it attacks the bishop on c4 and the pawn on e4. At this level, prioritize moves that create multiple problems for the opponent, especially when they improve your central structure or challenge an advanced pawn. Takeaway: Before choosing a quiet move, check whether a central pawn break creates tempo on a piece and a pawn at the same time.

Your move: Nc3. Nc3 is a good choice here. It develops your b1-knight and attacks the bishop on e4, so you improve a piece while creating a simple one-move threat. Takeaway: When you can develop a piece and attack an opponent’s piece at the same time, that is usually a strong beginner-friendly move.

://c1. Your Nc3 was a reasonable developing move, but it missed the more forcing chance: Nh4 attacks the queen on f5 and also defends the pawn on g2. Instead of only developing, you used a tempo to make Black respond to a direct queen attack. How to find it: When you have a developing move available, pause and run a quick forcing-move check: can one of your pieces attack the opponent’s queen, king, or another high-value piece while also improving your own position? Here, the knight on f3 can jump to h4 and attack the queen on f5, so you choose the move that develops with tempo instead of a quiet developing move. Takeaway: Before making a normal developing move, check whether you have a safe developing move with tempo.

Nbd2. Your Nc3 was a very natural developing move, but it missed the more forcing version of the same idea: Nbd2 develops the b1-knight while attacking the bishop on e4 and also defending c4 and Bf1. The key point is that Nc3 attacks d5 and Be4, but Nbd2 attacks Be4 while also adding useful defensive coverage, so it solves the central tension and improves coordination in one move instead of only creating pressure. How to find it: When two developing moves attack the same piece, do not choose automatically; compare the extra jobs each move performs. Here, list the candidate moves by their concrete effects: which one attacks the bishop on e4, which one defends important pieces or pawns, and which one improves the least active piece. Nbd2 wins that comparison because it develops the b1-knight, attacks Be4, and adds defense to c4 and Bf1, so it is the more multi-purpose move. Takeaway: When two moves create the same threat, prefer the one that also improves coordination or adds useful defense.
